## [00:00 - 03:00]

In the next two lessons we'll be looking at product market fit and market validation and tips and tricks and tools that we use to do this at scale. Now there's both free and paid methods of this and we're going to cover both. Now this is really important because actually you've done a lot of hard work up till now and a lot of it is probably based on assumptions, based on assumptions that you're pushed onto your users and you have to do that at a point to understand what it is that's actually making them convert. So you might be mentioning three or four different things and it might be that there's only two things in there that are important. Now if you could double down on those two things and leave out the two that aren't important you'll actually convert better. So over time the key to that success is actually in the customer database that you already have, you just need to go and work out what it is that they actually want to hear about and what it is that made them buy initially. So what we're calling this product market fit and scale is that you've already got a product that's in the market and it obviously fits in some way but you're trying to double down on understanding exactly what it is that has made, given that product, some success till now and you want to understand that better so you can better tailor your marketing, tailor your messaging, even maybe make changes and tailor the product if needed to better service this market. The better you can service the market, the closer you can get to telling them what they want to hear, the cheaper you'll be able to acquire those customers. So now we're going to cover some of the free ways to check out your product market fit and scale and learn what we can. So what I do is often just read our product reviews. So what I do is go to Google and it's up in QuadLock and the good thing about the Google reviews is if your product sold anywhere on the internet all the reviews will feed it back into here. So you don't just get the reviews from your website, you'll get reviews from all over the net so you can get a few more reviews. I'm going to click on iPhone bike kit that's got over 9,000 reviews and I'm going to look at the five star reviews and I'd start to read through these reviews and understand what exactly is it that people or customers love like, dislike about our product, specifically this QuadLock bike kit for iPhone X. So often what we've found is that there's points in here that people are finding very appealing about your product and reasons that they like it that you may not be talking about in your copy, in your conversion campaigns because you have written them off as features but maybe not the most important features. So a good example with us as QuadLock is reading reviews we've realised that people were continuously telling us just how good of protection they feel they're getting from their QuadLock case. So they've dropped in, it's protected phone really well and that it's not really the core

## [03:00 - 06:08]

purpose of our case but it's a massive reason to still have that case. So then what we did we looked at all our ads and all our content at the time and there was really not much about the case being an awesome protective case. So then what we did on all our videos, depending no matter what it was, we're a motorcycle, motorcycle, drive, at the end of that video we finished with the drop shot of an iPhone hitting the ground, bouncing and being fine after that because we found that one feature was a really important reason why people wanted to buy a QuadLock case. So once you've read your best reviews and find out what people love about your product it's always a good idea to go back and read your worst reviews and some of the reviews in the middle to find out what else it is that people are saying I like the product but this is no good or I mean a lot of the worst reviews, a lot of the one star reviews will be something along the lines of the product wasn't delivered or they put their address in wrong or something like that so they're not really that helpful when it comes to the product but when you read a four star review and you see something along the lines of I really like this product but XXXY that's where you go okay we can either address that or there's a way we can address that in the copy or the content and get people a better understanding or we can better the product so these are all good bits of information so the worst thing you can do is think oh there's bad reviews they're bad people no they're good people that just had a bad experience most of the time you want to understand that because it might be that hey what you've just found out here is the product's great but we're having delivery issues and we've noticed that it's always in Italy and if we know that we can go okay how can we fix delivery in Italy, products fine but it's the experience that the customer has been getting consistently that isn't that fine so you want to know that and if you can fix that and better that you're on your way to having a bigger better business. So another tool we often use is Google Trends and we use this to look for trends in the market but a lot of people use it for trends in the market but I think another great reason to use it is to understand what parts of the market are interested in what you're maybe potentially going to do or what you're already doing so if a good example here I've put in iPhone wireless charge you can see demand or interest is picked up over time but what's more interesting to me is to look at where these searches are coming from because if these searches are coming from markets that you're already strong in you've got good potential that you've got a new product that is already going to be popular in the markets you can see United Kingdom, United States, Australia, Singapore all markets we sell into at scale so we know when we have this product that will turn the quad lock into a wireless charger we've got good markets sitting there right now ready to go where people in that market that we already acquire customers from are going to be interested in that new product. So now we're going to talk about some paid methods for testing the product market fit at scale. One thing we've done before successfully is sold our existing iPhone bikeman product as an iPhone motorcycle product now the product was relatively good

## [06:08 - 07:27]

for both applications but it was not perfect for motorcycles so what we did is we put it up on a landing page sold it onto the site for motorcycles that it was good for just to judge if the market was interested in the product and the offering we had and the way we went about selling it and what we found is that very quickly we had a lot of people jump on board by that product specifically for the motor market they were very happy with it and we made $60,000 worth of sales in a very short period of time which then made us go okay we've got to go back to the drawing board we've got to find a new better way of making the quad lock specifically for motorcycle and now we have that product and it's been you know a hit worldwide. So to test this landing page approach with Facebook has what we do is we'd run the ads fairly wide a wide interest group and we'd start to measure the kind of the click-through rates the cost per click the conversion rate of what we get and the return on ad spend and if we're seeing the kind of signs that show okay that's something we can work with long term and we can build a market off that then we'll definitely look further out and it means okay we need a product just for this market. So now you can use some of these tools to validate your own products and look at your own markets and fill out the workbook below and I'll see you in the next lesson.
